preamble: The shift from the voiceless alveolar stop [t] to the postalveolar affricate [tʃ] is a primary form of palatalization. It frequently occurs via 'yod-coalescence', where a dental stop fuses with a following palatal glide [j] or front vowel (e.g., English 'nature' [t] + [j] > [tʃ]). This process is common in the development of Romance languages and in synchronic English speech.

examples.note: Historical yod-coalescence where the stop-glide sequence fused into an affricate.
Historical palatalization of the dental stop before a glide.
Allophonic palatalization before high front vowel /i/.

sources: Ladefoged, P., & Maddieson, I. (1996). The Sounds of the World's Languages.
Alkire, T., & Rosen, C. (2010). Romance Philology.
